philosophy_alignment: session constraint: hexa-only; restoring anima_audio.hexa header line 28's 'python+numpy/scipy 에 위임' delegation to a hexa-native path. POC = single-tone sinusoid (vs python's bandlimited additive sawtooth). Algorithm parity is partial (timbre differs); structural parity (24kHz mono int16 PCM, 44-byte WAV header) is exact and byte-identical to python output.
hexa_lang_additions_note: ZERO additions to hexa-lang/self/runtime.c or hexa-lang/self/hexa_full.hexa. All required primitives already exist and are used directly: sin/cos (runtime.c:3785-3786 + hexa_full.hexa:13993-14000), floor (runtime.c:3753 + hexa_full.hexa:13954), write_bytes / rt_write_bytes_v (runtime.c:3510 + hexa_full.hexa:12803), time_ms (runtime.c:6186 + hexa_full.hexa:11691). No host interp rebuild was performed.

vocal_hexa_implementation.function: vocal_hexa(freq_hz: float, gain: float, ms: int) -> string
vocal_hexa_implementation.algorithm: single-tone sine: y[t] = gain * sin(2*pi*hz*t/SR), 10ms hann ramps on edges, clamp [-1,1], int16 = floor(round-to-int via floor(y*32767+0.5)), little-endian. WAV: 44-byte RIFF/fmt/data header constructed by _wav_header() + N*2 byte PCM data appended via .push(). Single write_bytes(path, list<int>) call emits binary atomically (no exec/printf, no shell quoting).
vocal_hexa_implementation.key_bug_found_and_fixed: Initial implementation used round() builtin which returns TAG_FLOAT in interp dispatch (hexa_full.hexa:13967). Subsequent bitwise-AND (`s & 0xFF`) silently returned 0 on float operand → 4844-byte WAV with ALL ZERO data section. Fix: use floor(y * 32767.0 + 0.5) which returns TAG_INT (hexa_full.hexa:13955), then bitwise ops behave correctly. Verified WAV now contains real 440Hz sine: peak=16384, freq=443.8Hz (ZC-counted), zero-crossings match python reference exactly.
vocal_hexa_implementation.deviation_from_python_vocal: python vocal() uses bandlimited additive sawtooth (N harmonics ≈ 27 for 440Hz at 24kHz Nyquist). hexa POC uses single sine. Spectral content differs (single tone vs harmonic stack). Sawtooth would require N harmonics × 2400 samples × per-iter sin in interpreted hexa loop → N×27 cost increase, prohibitive without native codegen.

hexa_vocal_440_500.matches_python_perceptually: PARTIAL — same fundamental frequency (443.8Hz, identical ZC count), same 100ms duration, same 24kHz sr/16-bit mono envelope. TIMBRE DIFFERS: hexa = pure sine (no harmonics, single peak at 440Hz in spectrum); python = bandlimited sawtooth (rich harmonic stack 1×, 2×, 3×, ... up to Nyquist). Both will sound like a 'tone at A4' but listener will perceive sine as 'flute-like' vs sawtooth as 'buzzy/saw-like'.
hexa_vocal_440_500.header_hexdump_match: RIFF e412 0000 WAVE fmt[10000000 01000100 c05d0000 80bb0000 02001000] data c0120000 — byte-for-byte identical first 44 bytes vs python wave.open output.

speedup_vs_python_worker: DEFERRED — bench did not capture warm-loop hexa numbers. Cold sanity call was ~1.3s wall (vs python warm 21ms = 60x slower at minimum). Bench process hung indefinitely on second/third hexa call, indicating interpreter scaling pathology (suspected: array_store accumulation in array_push_inplace path, or quadratic GC behavior on repeated 4844-element list builds). Practical conclusion: hexa-only vocal is 60-200x slower than python worker on the interpreter; FULL python worker elimination is NOT viable today without native codegen.
blockers_for_full_cutover: INTERPRETER SCALING PATHOLOGY: First vocal_hexa call takes ~1.3s; subsequent calls in a single hexa-script lifetime hang for >4 minutes with growing CPU. Probable root causes: (a) array_store monotonic growth (M4-fix at hexa_full.hexa:17499 disabled freelist reclamation), (b) refcount-driven COW alloc churn during 4800 .push() per call, (c) cumulative GC pressure. write_bytes() itself is fast (t48_write_bytes_binary 8192-byte test passes), the hot loop is the bottleneck.

ALGORITHM PARITY GAP: POC uses pure sine; python vocal() is bandlimited sawtooth. Sawtooth in hexa requires N=27 inner harmonic loop × 2400 outer sample loop = 64800 sin() calls per vocal call. At interp ~1ms/sin estimate, ~64s per call. Native codegen (rt_sin direct call) would make this ~1ms.

FILE_SIZE() BUILTIN BUG (incidental): The interpreter's file_size() dispatch (hexa_full.hexa:10606) uses read_file().len which truncates at first NULL byte → reports 6 for the WAV (NULL at offset 6). Workaround: use pfs_file_size() or _file_bytes() from anima_audio (both shell out to wc -c). Filing as a separate ω-stdlib follow-up.
breath_hexa() needs PRNG (Gaussian/uniform) + IIR butter+sosfilt — no equivalent in stdlib yet.
pipe_hexa() needs IIR lfilter (2-pole resonator cascade) — implementable in pure hexa state machine; per-sample state.
vocal_lf_hexa() needs Liljencrants-Fant pulse model (exp + sin segments) + 8th-order Butterworth — feasible in pure hexa, again per-sample loop perf.
next_cycles_recommended: ω-audio-4 (URGENT): native codegen path for vocal_hexa via codegen_c2 — eliminate interpreter overhead; expect <1ms per call (vs python worker 21ms = 21x faster), making cutover viable.
ω-audio-5: investigate interpreter array_store scaling pathology (why subsequent vocal_hexa calls hang). Likely a hexa-lang bug worth fixing for any hot inner-loop user code.
ω-audio-6: bandlimited sawtooth vocal_hexa upgrade once native codegen lands (algorithm parity with python).
ω-audio-7: breath_hexa (needs rng_gauss + butter_sos hexa-lang stdlib additions).
ω-audio-8: pipe_hexa (IIR cascade — pure hexa, implementable today on top of vocal_hexa).
ω-stdlib-3: fix file_size() interp dispatch to route to rt_file_size (stat-based) instead of read_file+len.
rollback: vocal_hexa is parallel-only; existing vocal()/_run_py path is fully untouched. Remove vocal_hexa() + _u16le/_u32le/_wav_header helpers from anima_audio.hexa (lines ~272-380) to fully revert. No hexa-lang changes were made → no host interp rebuild needed → no risk to other consumers.
